original_hypothesis.title: USD Price Feed Distortion via Single Price Array Application
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The proposeUSDPrice function takes an array of token contracts but only a single USD price. When executed, this single price is applied to all tokens in the array, potentially causing massive mispricing if tokens with different values are bundled.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: If operators attempt to update WETH and USDC in the same proposal, one will be drastically mispriced, distorting the getLockedWeightedValue calculation which drives protocol rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: A single proposedPrice is applied in a loop to all tokenContract addresses in the proposal array

preconditions: Price feed operators propose a price update for multiple tokens with different actual USD values in a single transaction

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Operator error or malicious proposal bundles high-value and low-value tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users holding the artificially inflated token receive vastly disproportionate rewards

economic_questions: How does getLockedWeightedValue impact the downstream schnibbles emission?
Can operators be tricked into bundling tokens?
validation_requirements: Simulate a bundled proposal and measure the impact on getLockedWeightedValue

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Existing locked positions in the mispriced token
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: None for the user (relies on operator action)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Thousands of times normal reward rate (e.g., if USDC is priced at WETH value)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until a new price proposal corrects it

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe distortion of protocol economy and unfair reward distribution

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Holders of the correctly priced or undervalued tokens

validation_result.mechanism: Single price applied to multiple tokens
validation_result.impact_summary: Bundling tokens with different market values in a single price update proposal assigns them all the same USD price, severely distorting weighted value calculations and reward distributions.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `proposeUSDPrice` function is designed to accept a single `_price` value alongside an array of `_contracts`. When the proposal reaches the required approval threshold, `_execUSDPriceUpdate` iterates through the `contracts` array and assigns the exact same `proposedPrice` to every token. 

Compounding this issue is a strict concurrency limit: the contract only allows one active proposal at a time (`if (usdUpdateProposal.proposer != address(0)) revert ProposalInProgressError();`). This means operators cannot propose price updates for different tokens simultaneously. They must either coordinate a 3-of-5 multisig approval for each token sequentially (which is operationally slow and risks stale prices) or bundle multiple tokens into a single proposal. If operators mistakenly bundle tokens with different actual values (e.g., WETH and USDB) to save time, the single price will be applied to all of them, drastically mispricing the assets and corrupting the `getLockedWeightedValue` function that relies on `usdPrice`.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code explicitly demonstrates that a single scalar price is stored in the proposal and subsequently applied to an array of token addresses in a loop, confirming the structural defect in the oracle update mechanism.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function accepts a single `_price` but an array of `_contracts`. It also enforces that only one proposal can be active at a time, forcing sequential updates or bundling.
The loop iterates over all contracts in the proposal and assigns the exact same `proposedPrice` to each one, regardless of their actual market value.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: An operator calls `proposeUSDPrice` with a single price and an array of multiple token addresses (e.g., WETH and USDB). The proposal is stored in `usdUpdateProposal`. Other operators call `approveUSDPrice`. Once `APPROVE_THRESHOLD` is reached, `approveUSDPrice` calls `_execUSDPriceUpdate()`. Inside `_execUSDPriceUpdate`, a `for` loop iterates over `usdUpdateProposal.contracts` and sets `configuredTokens[tokenContract].usdPrice = usdUpdateProposal.proposedPrice` for every token, mispricing any token whose actual value differs from the single proposed price.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario relies on operator error induced by a flawed API and strict concurrency limits. Operators, unable to propose multiple prices concurrently, bundle WETH and USDB into a single proposal with a price of 3000e18. The multisig approves the proposal. USDB is now priced at $3000 instead of $1. A user locks USDB and their `getLockedWeightedValue` is artificially inflated by 3000x, allowing them to drain a disproportionate share of protocol rewards.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Severe distortion of the protocol's economic accounting. The `getLockedWeightedValue` function drives reward distributions; artificially inflating a token's USD price allows holders of that token to steal rewards from other users.
recommended_mitigations: Modify `proposeUSDPrice` to accept an array of prices `uint256[] calldata _prices` that corresponds 1:1 with the `_contracts` array.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockManager and configure two tokens: WETH and USDB. 2. Call `proposeUSDPrice` with `_price = 3000e18` and `_contracts = [WETH, USDB]`. 3. Have the required number of operators call `approveUSDPrice`. 4. Call `getConfiguredToken` for USDB and observe that its `usdPrice` is incorrectly set to 3000e18.
